one_line_takeaway: AGENTWARD is a conceptual, defense-in-depth architecture (not an empirically evaluated system) that assigns a distinct security layer to each of five agent-lifecycle stages and coordinates them via shared risk state, demonstrated only through two qualitative attack-chain case studies on an OpenClaw prototype with no quantitative detection or overhead results.
key_finding: The paper does not report a quantitative finding (no accuracy/precision/recall/attack-success-rate numbers). Its substantive claim, argued through the two case studies rather than measured, is that single-stage/pointwise defenses (e.g., input filtering alone) are insufficient because attacks can propagate across stages and become durable (e.g., via memory persistence), whereas coordinating weak, individually-insufficient signals across layers (an initialization warning, a decision-stage plan-alignment check, an execution-stage permission tightening) allows the system to intercept attacks that any single layer would miss or only partially catch. Case 1 (malicious skill -> unauthorized data access) shows Foundation Scan flagging a capability/content mismatch as an inconclusive risk marker that Decision Alignment later uses to detect plan drift beyond the authorized task, with Execution Control then applying stricter permissions to block the final sensitive-file read/exfiltration attempt. Case 2 (indirect prompt injection -> persistent backdoor and DoS) shows Input Sanitization flagging suspicious tool-result content that 'detection-only' filtering would let through, with Cognition Protection then blocking the write of the injected instructions into durable memory files (MEMORY.md/AGENTS.md), and Execution Control serving as a final backstop against a harmful command pattern if the memory-based persistence path were bypassed.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution is AGENTWARD, a lifecycle-oriented, defense-in-depth architecture for autonomous LLM agents that decomposes the agent runtime into five stages (initialization, input, memory, decision, execution) and maps a dedicated protection layer onto each: Foundation Scan (initialization), Input Sanitization (input), Cognition Protection (memory), Decision Alignment (decision), and Execution Control (execution). The architecture is built on four design principles the authors state explicitly: full-lifecycle coverage, zero-trust enforcement (no layer trusts an upstream 'allow' decision), heterogeneous defense mechanisms across layers (to avoid correlated bypass), and cross-layer coordination via a shared security context that carries risk evidence, trust annotations, and an 'attack-path knowledge base' forward across stages and sessions. The authors implement a plugin-native prototype on top of the OpenClaw agent runtime, attaching the five layers to specific runtime hooks (Foundation Scan to before_prompt_build; Input Sanitization and Decision Alignment to before_message_write for tool-role and assistant-role messages respectively; Cognition Protection and Execution Control to before_tool_call, the former only on memory-file writes, the latter on all tool calls). The current prototype's scope is limited to malicious-skill/misconfiguration checks at init, tool-result contamination detection at input, memory-file modification anomaly detection at cognition, LLM-judge-based response review at decision, and pattern-based dangerous-command interception at execution.
threat_model: The paper frames the threat model as cross-stage propagation: an attack can enter the agent lifecycle at any of the five stages (e.g., a poisoned skill at initialization, indirect prompt injection via a webpage/tool response at input) and, if not intercepted locally, persist through intermediate state (e.g., by being written into durable memory such as MEMORY.md/AGENTS.md) before eventually manifesting as harmful execution (unsafe command execution, unauthorized file/service modification, data exfiltration, denial-of-service). The design assumes a zero-trust, worst-case posture in which upstream components, retained state, and external signals may already be compromised, so each layer independently evaluates local inputs/state/behavior rather than deferring to an earlier layer's allow decision.
limitations: Limitations stated or evident in the paper's own text: (1) No quantitative evaluation — the authors explicitly disclaim benchmark totals and present only two illustrative case studies, so there are no detection-rate, false-positive-rate, latency/overhead, or attack-success-rate numbers to substantiate the architecture's effectiveness. (2) The prototype's actual mechanisms are narrower than the full conceptual design: e.g., Decision Alignment is implemented only as 'LLM-judge-based response review' and Execution Control only as 'pattern-based dangerous-command interception,' which are much simpler than the fuller set of mechanisms (intent-consistency checking, inference-logic review, sensitive-parameter validation, least-privilege enforcement, eBPF monitoring, etc.) described conceptually in Section III. (3) The architecture is tied to one specific agent runtime (OpenClaw) and its plugin hook system (before_prompt_build, before_message_write, before_tool_call), so generality to other agent frameworks is asserted but not demonstrated. (4) No adversarial robustness testing against adaptive attackers who specifically target the coordination mechanism itself (e.g., attacks designed to stay just under each layer's individual detection threshold while evading cross-layer accumulation) is reported. (5) No discussion of the performance/latency cost of running five inspection layers on every message/tool call, nor of false-positive burden on legitimate agent workflows.
